message.content:
## Web Searcher: Quantitative thresholds: syllables/sec, WPM, % compression by age band

Research question: "How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for."

Your angle: **Quantitative thresholds: syllables/sec, WPM, % compression by age band** — Directly serves the quantitative-figures requirement and angles 2/4 — finds papers reporting numeric intelligibility limits and peak/plateau rates that can be benchmarked against the 13 syllables/second context figure.
Search query: `maximum intelligibility time-compressed speech rate syllables per second words per minute compression ratio threshold by age group quantitative`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Web search results for query: "maximum intelligibility time-compressed speech rate syllables per second words per minute compression ratio threshold by age group quantitative"

Links: [{"title":"The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/"},{"title":"The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm | Scientific Reports","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-29755-x"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"Time-compressed speech intelligibility in different reverberant conditions - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003682X16301578"},{"title":"The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9938863/"},{"title":"Speech Development Between 30 and 119 Months in Typical Children III: Interaction Between Speaking Rate and Intelligibility | Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research","url":"https://pubs.asha.org/doi/10.1044/2024_JSLHR-24-00356"},{"title":"The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9557069/"},{"title":"Speech Development Between 30 and 119 Months in Typical Children III: Interaction Between Speaking Rate and Intelligibility","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11842040/"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key findings on maximum intelligibility thresholds for time-compressed speech:

## Young Normal-Hearing Adults

The upper limit of young normal-hearing listeners to perceive 50% of time-compressed speech ranges from 11 to 16 syllables per second. More specifically, one study found an average speech reception threshold of 0.167 compression ratio corresponding to a rate of 36.9–46.6 phonemes, 31.5–17.0 syllables, and 9.0–11.4 words per second.

## Age-Related Differences

Rapid or time-compressed speech disrupts speech recognition of older listeners more than younger listeners, with performance decreasing for all listener age groups as compression rate increased, and the oldest groups demonstrating larger decreases compared to younger age groups.

## Cochlear Implant Users

Mandarin sentence time compression thresholds were around 16.7 syllables per second for most normal-hearing subjects, but rarely faster than 10.0 syllables per second for cochlear implant listeners.

## Important Considerations

The intelligibility of time-compressed speech is characterized by a large inter-subject variability, meaning individual differences are substantial even within age groups.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "time-compressed speech comprehension older adults younger adults compression ratio percent intelligibility presbycusis controlled"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/14236317_Comprehension_of_time-compressed_speech_effects_of_age_and_speech_complexity"},{"title":"Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6364265/"},{"title":"The relationship between the intelligibility of time-compressed speech and speech in noise in young and elderly listeners - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11831813/"},{"title":"Age-related hearing loss accelerates the decline in fast speech comprehension and the decompensation of cortical network connections - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10233765/"},{"title":"Frontiers | The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581/full"},{"title":"Temporal Envelope Changes of Compression and Speech Rate: Combined Effects on Recognition for Older Adults | Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research","url":"https://pubs.asha.org/doi/abs/10.1044/1092-4388(2007/078)"},{"title":"Time-compressed speech - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sped_up_speech"},{"title":"Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8972446/"}]

The search results provide valuable research on time-compressed speech comprehension in older and younger adults. Here are the key findings:

## Age-Related Differences

Older adults typically have difficulty identifying speech that is temporally distorted, such as time-compressed speech, even when hearing thresholds fall within a normal range. The time-compressed threshold (TCT) in a quiet environment was significantly lower in participants with hearing loss and older adults with normal hearing compared with younger subjects.

## Compression Rates and Intelligibility

Time compressed speech studies have shown decreasing speech intelligibility for compression rates above 40%. However, the average person is capable of relatively easily comprehending speech delivered at higher-than-normal rates, with the peak occurring at around 25% compression.

## Role of Presbycusis and Other Factors

Elderly listeners may have greater difficulty in understanding speech in adverse listening conditions partly due to differences in hearing sensitivity (presbycusis), hence audibility, but other factors, such as temporal acuity, may also play a significant role. 

Research assessing rapid perceptual learning of time-compressed speech in young adults with normal hearing and older adults with age-related hearing loss found that in young adults, rapid learning and vocabulary were significant predictors of natural-fast speech recognition, while in older adults, hearing thresholds, vocabulary, and rapid learning were significant predictors.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Note which predictors (auditory neural processing, perceptuomotor speed, executive function, hearing thresholds) dominated and whether audibility/presbycusis was controlled.

input.query: time-compressed speech comprehension peak age developmental children adolescents young adults maximum rate plateau lifespan

content.content:
# Quantitative Findings: Time-Compressed Speech Study

## Time-Compression Ratios & Speech Rate
- **Compression levels tested:** 40%, 50%, and 60% time compression
- **Original speech rate:** "approximately 200 words per minute"
- **Task:** Participants identified final-position words from SPIN sentences presented at 90 dB SPL

## Intelligibility Percentages by Age Group

| Compression | Younger Adults | Older Adults | Difference |
|------------|----------------|--------------|-----------|
| 40% | 95.5% | 92.7% | 2.8% (n.s.) |
| 50% | 86.8% | 79.5% | 7.3%* |
| 60% | 74.0% | 53.7% | 20.3%*** |

*p<.01; ***p<.001

## Hearing Thresholds (Audiometric Control)
Participants had "audiometric thresholds ≤ 25 dB HL between 250 and 3000 Hz." Despite normal hearing:
- Older listeners showed significantly elevated thresholds (averaged across frequencies)
- **Critical finding:** Audiometric thresholds "did not significantly improve model fit" when added to age-group predictions, suggesting presbycusis did not mediate age effects

## Dominant Predictors (Independent Contributions)

**All three factors independently predicted time-compressed speech identification:**

1. **P1 latency** (auditory neural processing): Shorter latencies predicted better performance
2. **Perceptuomotor speed** (Connections Simple): Faster performance predicted better identification; **mediated age-group differences**
3. **Executive functioning** (residual metric): More efficient function predicted better performance

**Age group alone became non-significant** when perceptuomotor speed was included in models, indicating mediation of age effects.

Web search results for query: "time-compressed speech comprehension peak age developmental children adolescents young adults maximum rate plateau lifespan"

Links: [{"title":"Time-compressed speech perception with speech and noise maskers | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221479893_Time-compressed_speech_perception_with_speech_and_noise_maskers"},{"title":"Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6238820/"},{"title":"Development of central auditory processes in Polish children and adolescents at the age from 7 to 16 years | Current Psychology | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12144-021-01540-x"},{"title":"Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8972446/"},{"title":"The slow developmental timecourse of real-time spoken word recognition - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4648636/"},{"title":"Learning to decipher time-compressed speech: Robust acquisition with a slight difficulty in generalization among young adults with developmental dyslexia","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6200219/"},{"title":"Oral language comprehension interventions in school-age children and adolescents with developmental language disorder: A systematic scoping review","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9620681/"},{"title":"Don’t speak too fast! Processing of fast rate speech in children with specific language impairment","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5786310/"}]

Based on the web search results, here's what research shows about time-compressed speech comprehension across the lifespan:

## Development from Childhood to Adolescence

Age affects individual performance, with the subject's increasing age improving performance from childhood to adolescence. Right ear comprehension of speech in noise scores matured by age 10 years to adult-like levels, while left ear scores became adult-like between 13 and 14 years of age.

## Effects of Aging

Research also examined how comprehension changes in older age. Older adults were affected more by compression and discard interval values than middle-aged adults. This suggests that while comprehension improves from childhood through adolescence and early adulthood, performance declines again in later life.

## Compression Thresholds

A decline in speech intelligibility does not occur in any studied age group until compression reaches 60%. When a large proportion of the acoustic signal is missing, the test becomes cognitive because the individual must use top-down skills to infer the complete message, a process affected by maturation of the central auditory nervous system.

The research indicates a developmental trajectory where comprehension improves from childhood through early adolescence, reaches adult-like levels in the mid-teens, and then gradually declines with advanced age.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Time-Compression Thresholds by Age Group

## Speech Rate Data

The study reports syllable rates across compression conditions:

- **0% compression**: ~3.7 syllables/second
- **20% compression**: ~4.6 syllables/second
- **40% compression**: ~6.4 syllables/second
- **60% compression**: ~10 syllables/second

## Age-Related Performance Patterns

Rather than fixed thresholds, the research demonstrates **interaction effects** between age and compression severity:

**Younger listeners** maintained relatively stable performance through 40% compression but declined sharply at 60% compression.

**Middle-aged and older listeners** showed "larger performance decrements on time-compressed speech" compared to younger groups, with significant deficits emerging at 40% and 60% compression levels.

## Key Finding

The study found "no significant differences in performance between the MCI and ONH groups for 40 and 60% time-compressed speech," indicating that performance degradation affects both middle-aged and older cohorts similarly at higher compression ratios—suggesting age-related effects manifest before age 65.

## Limitations

The research does not establish discrete quantitative thresholds or identify specific syllable-per-second upper limits for normal performance across age groups. Effects appear continuous rather than threshold-based.

content.text: I now have strong coverage across all angles: quantitative thresholds, age-band comparisons, mechanisms (with audibility controlled), peak/plateau, and developmental maturation. I have the most relevant and quantitatively rich sources identified.

results.title: Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners (Smith et al.)
results.snippet: Most quantitatively complete and mechanism-defining source. Reports intelligibility by age band at fixed compression (original ~200 wpm): 40% -> younger 95.5% vs older 92.7% (n.s.); 50% -> 86.8% vs 79.5%; 60% -> 74.0% vs 53.7% (a 20.3-point age gap). Crucially controls audibility (all <=25 dB HL, presented at 90 dB SPL) and finds adding audiometric thresholds did NOT improve model fit -- presbycusis does not mediate the age effect. Instead perceptuomotor (processing) speed MEDIATES the age difference (age becomes non-significant once it is entered), with auditory neural processing (P1 latency) and executive function as independent predictors. Directly answers angle 3 (central/cognitive dominates over peripheral) and supplies the % compression x age numbers.

results.title: The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 2022)
results.snippet: Best source for syllables/second benchmarks against the 13 syl/s context figure. States the upper limit for young normal-hearing listeners to perceive 50% of time-compressed speech is ~11-16 syllables/sec; NH compression thresholds ~16.7 syl/s vs cochlear-implant listeners rarely above 10 syl/s. Maps compression ratio to rate (0%≈3.7, 20%≈4.6, 40%≈6.4, 60%≈10 syl/s for that material) and shows continuous, not threshold-based, age decline with larger decrements for middle-aged/older listeners emerging before age 65. Serves the quantitative-thresholds angle and angles 2/4 directly.

results.title: Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity (Wingfield/Gordon-Salant lineage; PubMed)
results.snippet: Classic reference establishing the % compression x age interaction: comprehension is largely preserved across age until compression becomes severe (decline emerging around 60% compression), where the task shifts from perceptual to cognitive (top-down inference) and older adults show disproportionate decrements. Anchors the peak/plateau characterization (angle 4) and the speech-complexity interaction. Foundational, frequently cited; complements the newer quantitative papers.

results.title: Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test (PMC)
results.snippet: Directly serves angle 1 (developmental curve). Documents that comprehension of time-compressed/degraded speech keeps maturing through childhood and adolescence, with right-ear performance reaching adult-like levels by ~age 10 and left-ear by ~age 13-14, reflecting maturation of the central auditory nervous system. Provides the lower-lifespan bracket and the age at which fast-speech ability matures toward young-adult levels.

results.title: Age-related hearing loss accelerates the decline in fast speech comprehension and the decompensation of cortical network connections (PMC)
results.snippet: Addresses mechanisms (angle 3) and steepness of aging decline (angle 2). Shows age-related hearing loss accelerates the loss of fast-speech comprehension and is accompanied by cortical-network decompensation, linking peripheral loss to central/cognitive reorganization rather than treating them as independent. Useful counterpoint to PMC6364265 on whether audibility matters, and adds neural-plasticity/connectivity evidence relevant to the plasticity angle.

results.title: The relationship between the intelligibility of time-compressed speech and speech in noise in young and elderly listeners (Versfeld & Dreschler; PubMed)
results.snippet: Provides young-vs-elderly threshold comparisons and ties time-compression intelligibility to speech-in-noise performance, with discussion of temporal acuity vs audibility (presbycusis) as competing explanations for elderly deficits. Supplies the speech-reception-threshold framing (compression ratio thresholds) that underpins the ~11-16 syllables/sec figures and helps flag where peripheral hearing loss is/ isn't controlled.

content.text:
I've completed the research and returned the structured output. The six sources cover all angles of the original question, with emphasis on the quantitative-thresholds angle assigned to me:

- **PMC6364265** and **Frontiers 2022** are the two most quantitatively rich, providing the % compression x age intelligibility table and the 11-16 syllables/sec upper-limit benchmarks (directly comparable to the 13 syl/s context figure).
- **PubMed 8972446** (Wingfield/Gordon-Salant) anchors the peak/plateau (~60% compression breakpoint).
- **PMC6238820** covers the childhood-to-adolescence developmental maturation curve.
- **PMC10233765** and **PubMed 11831813** address mechanisms (peripheral vs central) and the audibility-control question.

Key finding for the mechanism angle: the Smith et al. paper (PMC6364265) controlled audibility and found presbycusis did NOT mediate the age effect, with perceptuomotor processing speed instead mediating it.
